topic: Named pose and action archetypes for tip menus
summary: Top-earning performers give their poses names — creates shared vocabulary between performer and room, makes the menu feel like a collection viewers can 'own' through tipping, and lets the performer chain moves into memorable sequences. The underlying logic is burlesque: transform the everyday act of posing/moving into a theatrical event with its own iconography. These named categories aren't sex-act choreography — they're performance-craft pose archetypes borrowed from photography, burlesque, and established cam-community patterns.
keyPoints: Affection-tier gestures (1–100 tokens each, drive chat velocity): blow kiss, wink, wave, hair toss, spin, cheek squeeze, lip bite, finger-gun, eyebrow raise, shoulder shrug + smile, 'say my name'

Pose archetypes (50–200 tokens each — name them for recall): 'Ariel' = lying on stomach with chin propped on hands; 'Goddess' = standing arms raised overhead; 'Kitten' = on all fours looking over shoulder; 'Pin-up' = seated leaning back on hands with legs to one side; 'Statue' = full-profile 45° turn with head back to camera; 'Contemplation' = seated cross-legged looking away; 'Window' = leaning against doorframe/prop
Outfit-interaction actions (100–300 tokens): shoes off, stockings down, glove peel (30–60s each), hair up/down, robe drop, turn-around reveal of back, open-then-close jacket/robe
Prop-play actions (100–400 tokens): feather boa tease, fan dance segment, silk-scarf reveal, mirror pose (performer faces away, viewer sees front in a mirror), bed-sheet peek

Interaction actions (varies): sing a line, read a fan's message aloud, toast/cheers, 30-second countdown game, spin-the-wheel, dice-roll (number determines next menu item), message-in-a-bottle (pick a folded note from a jar)

Premium craft-set pieces (500–2000+): 3-song burlesque-style sequence, photoset posedown (slow cycle through 5–7 signature poses), private-room GFE touch (girlfriend-experience conversational warmth with no explicit content beyond the performer's set comfort)

Naming convention: borrow from mythology, fairy tales, or your persona's theme — a goth performer might name poses 'Raven', 'Reliquary', 'Vespers'; a girl-next-door persona might use 'Sunday', 'Morning', 'Porch Swing'
Use token amount to signal category to the room: affection ends in 5 or 11 (5, 25, 55); pose ends in 7 (77, 127, 177); outfit ends in 0 (100, 200, 300). Regulars learn the language fast
Chain poses into scripted 'sets' — e.g. 'Sunrise Set' (3 poses at 200 tokens triggers all in sequence); bundles lift average order value vs. single-pose tips
Every performer should have 3 signature poses that are 'theirs' — a regular should be able to recognize them from a still frame even with the face cropped
